rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. The published Texas rank of 18 is an artefact of that renumbering and carries no meaning. Texas must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
score_delta: +24.7 (published 14.1 to proposed 38.8). This delta corrects a data defect and does NOT represent institutional improvement. The published Texas entry was a bulk-import placeholder with last_assessed null and no evidentiary basis whatsoever. This assessment is the first evidence-based baseline for Texas and must not be reported or communicated as a score increase, as a change in Texas conduct, or as a trend.
band_change: critical to Developing. Same caveat as score_delta: the band change reflects the replacement of an unevidenced placeholder with a first evidence-based baseline, not measured improvement in Texas institutional conduct.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt, and explicitly not anchored to the published composite of 14.1, which is a bulk-import placeholder with last_assessed null and no evidentiary basis. Note the anchoring risk for Texas runs opposite to the rest of this wave: the Texas placeholder is deflated rather than inflated, so the discipline required was to resist scoring Texas low because a broken index and a politically salient reputation both pointed there. Calibrated to the methodology anchors and to the 17 states assessed at full depth under this methodology: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, North Carolina 45.0, Alaska 47.5, Oregon 50.6, Colorado 52.5, Wisconsin 53.1, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, Virginia 55.6, Maryland 55.6, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0, New Jersey 60.6. Texas at 38.8 sits between Ohio 38.1 and North Carolina 45.0, above Arizona 36.2. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":38.8,"band":"Developing","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 3.0, EMP 2.2, ACT 2.8, EQU 1.8, BND 2.2, ACC 3.0, SYS 2.8, INT 2.6. baseComposite 38.75; stdDev 0.409 so consistencyMult 1.0; 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0.0; final composite 38.8, band Developing. The composite sits 1.2 points below the Developing/Functional boundary at 40, which is outside the 1.0-point instability threshold but close enough to flag. Three sensitivity cases were also run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: raising EQU from 1.8 to 2.8 returns {"composite":41.9,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; raising ACC from 3.0 to 4.0 returns {"composite":41.9,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; adding 0.5 to every dimension returns {"composite":51.3,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. A one-point move in any single dimension would flip the band, so the Developing classification is firm but not comfortable.
attribution_note: Scores Texas state conduct only, across all three branches and statewide elected offices, since the benchmark scores the jurisdiction rather than an administration. Texas was under unified Republican control for the whole 2021-2026 window with Greg Abbott as governor throughout, so no divided-government attribution problem arises. Federal policy imposed on Texas is excluded from scoring; what is scored is state discretion. The decision not to expand Medicaid is scored as state conduct because expansion is a state option: multiple expansion bills were introduced and failed in the 2025 session. The USDA threat to suspend 8.4 million dollars is federal enforcement; what is scored is the state administrative failure that triggered it, namely more than 90,000 backlogged applications and 38-day average determinations against a 30-day standard. The federal prison heat litigation is scored on established record facts only: the March 2025 unconstitutionality finding, the confirmed falsified temperature logs, TDCJ testimony that full cooling costs 1.5 billion dollars, and the legislature's discretionary appropriation of 118 million dollars. The April 2026 trial had produced no ruling as of the assessment date and no outcome is predicted or scored. In M.D. v. Abbott the Fifth Circuit vacated the contempt order in February 2025 and removed the district judge, so contempt is not scored as an established finding against the state; the June 2026 court monitor reports covering 2023-2024 activity are used instead, and the foster care lag is disclosed as an evidence gap. National Guard deaths on Operation Lone Star are scored under B1 and I3 because the deployment was a state-directed mission under state command. Uvalde is scored narrowly on state conduct: DPS personnel outcomes and the department's continued withholding of records into August 2025 after local agencies had released theirs. Local and school-district police conduct is not scored as state conduct.
key_evidence: Texas has the highest uninsured rate in the United States at 16.7 percent, and 21.6 percent among working-age adults. Roughly 42 percent of everyone in the national Medicaid coverage gap lives in Texas, an estimated 617,000 adults. Multiple expansion bills failed in the 2025 session.

As of March 2026, more than 198,000 people were on six Texas Health and Human Services Commission interest lists for home and community-based care, with waits of 17 to 18 years. HHSC officials confirmed there is no eligibility evaluation before someone joins a list.

The Sunset Advisory Commission has reviewed Texas state agencies on a statutory cycle since 1977, evaluating 20 to 30 of roughly 130 entities every two-year cycle and publishing critical staff findings. The State Auditor publishes classified turnover annually: 15.4 percent in fiscal 2025, down from 16.5 percent, including a 21.8 percent rate in one salary schedule.

After 139 people died in the July 2025 Hill Country floods, Senate Bill 3 was signed on 2025-09-05, funding sirens, forecasting and a swift water rescue training centre. Kerr County installed and tested sirens, and when flooding returned to the same region in July 2026 the reported death toll was 2.

ERCOT has completed five winters and three summers of mandatory weatherization inspections since the 2021 grid collapse, in which unplanned outages averaged 34 GW. In Winter Storm Fern in 2026, non-IBR outages tracked at or below Winter Storm Elliott levels.

Texas reported more than 90,000 backlogged food-assistance applications, the oldest from August 2023, and a 38-day average eligibility determination against a 30-day federal standard, prompting a USDA threat to suspend nearly 8.4 million dollars.

A federal judge found Texas prison heat conditions plainly unconstitutional in March 2025 and called a TDCJ temperature log a fabricated document; an internal investigation then confirmed falsification. TDCJ testified in March 2026 that full cooling would cost 1.5 billion dollars. The legislature appropriated 118 million dollars.

Texas voters approved Proposition 4 in November 2025, dedicating up to 1 billion dollars of sales tax revenue a year from 2027 to 2047 to the Texas Water Fund, the largest water investment in the state's 180-year history.
House Bill 2 delivered 8.5 billion dollars to public schools in 2025, including 4.2 billion for pay and nearly 2 billion restructuring special education onto a service-intensity funding model.

The Texas rainy day fund is projected to reach 28.5 billion dollars by August 2027 and has hit its statutory cap, while disability interest lists grew and roughly two-thirds of prison units remained without full air conditioning.

evidence.quote: Texas leads in uninsured rates: 16.7% lack insurance, the nation's highest rate
evidence.claim: primary support for EQ1 Universality at 1 and S4 Structural Critique at 1

evidence.quote: Texas accounts for roughly 42% of all people in the coverage gap nationally
evidence.claim: scale of state-chosen exclusion; supports EQ1 and B4 Refusal Ethics

evidence.quote: HHSC officials said there's no evaluation of eligibility before people join interest lists, so some people end up being ineligible for services
evidence.claim: people invest years without being told whether help can ever come; drives B3 Scope Clarity to 1

evidence.quote: As of March 2026, there are more than 198,000 people across six HHSC interest lists for home and community-based services
evidence.claim: primary support for E1 Affective Resonance, EQ2 and B2 Autonomy Preservation at 2

evidence.quote: People can wait 17 to 18 years for programs like the Community Living Assistance and Support Services Waiver
evidence.claim: magnitude of the wait; supports AC2 Proportionality

evidence.quote: The statewide turnover rate for classified, regular full- and part-time employees for fiscal year 2025 was 15.4 percent, down from 16.5 percent the previous fiscal year
evidence.claim: genuine published workforce tracking with an improving trend; primary support for B1 and I2 Non-Performance

evidence.quote: During each two-year legislative cycle, the Sunset Commission evaluates about 20-30 of the approximately 130 entities subject to Sunset review
evidence.claim: statutory institutional self-review running since 1977; primary support for A3, AB3 at 4, I2 and I5

evidence.quote: records will no longer be allowed to be kept secret from the public just because a suspect is dead
evidence.claim: 2023 closure of the dead suspect loophole, a transparency gain that cuts against expectation; supports AB3 at 4

evidence.quote: ERCOT has completed five winters and three summers of weatherization inspections, which contribute to a high level of compliance with the PUCT Weather Emergency Preparedness rule
evidence.claim: sustained structural change after the 2021 failure; primary support for A5, AB4 at 4 and I1

evidence.quote: Winter Storm Fern in 2026 continued this encouraging trend, with Non-IBR outages tracking very close to - or below - those of Winter Storm Elliott
evidence.claim: measured outcome showing the reform worked; supports AC3 Efficacy

evidence.quote: As flooding returns to Hill Country, Texas lawmakers say new safety laws already making a difference
evidence.claim: the July 2026 flood is the first live test of the post-2025 regime; primary support for AC3 and AB4

evidence.quote: Texas Water Development Board accelerates Senate Bill 3 implementation plan and grant funding agreements
evidence.claim: follow-through inside twelve months on a named commitment; supports AC5 and S5

evidence.quote: This amendment dedicates up to $1 billion of sales tax revenue per year from 2027-2047 to the Texas Water Fund
evidence.claim: binding 20-year horizon; primary support for S2 Long-Term Impact at 4 and I1

evidence.quote: Texas voters approved Proposition 4, which authorizes $20 billion to be spent on water projects over the next two decades
evidence.claim: largest water investment in state history; supports AC4 and S1

evidence.quote: House Bill 2 includes $4.2 billion for pay raises and nearly $2 billion to overhaul special education
evidence.claim: largest needs-linked allocation in the window; supports AC2 and EQ2

evidence.quote: Funding is now based on the type and intensity of services students receive, with $834 million allocated and $250 million for transition support
evidence.claim: genuine differentiation by need rather than headcount; supports A2 Contextual Sensitivity

evidence.quote: Texas reported more than 90,000 backlogged SNAP applications, with the oldest from August 2023
evidence.claim: core responsiveness failure; primary support for AC1 at 2, E1 at 2 and B3 at 1

evidence.quote: the USDA is threatening to suspend nearly $8.4 million if HHSC does not come into compliance with federal SNAP requirements
evidence.claim: independent federal confirmation of the administrative failure; supports AC1 and EQ4

evidence.quote: the plainly unconstitutional treatment of some of the most vulnerable, marginalized members of our society
evidence.claim: judicial finding on state conduct; primary support for AB1 at 2 and S4 at 1

evidence.quote: An internal investigation then confirmed that temperature logs were falsified in at least one state prison
evidence.claim: acknowledgment followed external establishment rather than preceding it; drives AB1 and E4 to 2

evidence.quote: lawmakers did provide $118 million for its installation
evidence.claim: repair is gestural relative to the $1.5 billion TDCJ-confirmed need; primary support for AB5 at 2

evidence.quote: TDCJ's estimated cost to fully cool Texas prisons rises to $1.5B
evidence.claim: establishes the scale against which the $118 million appropriation is judged; supports AB2 and AB5

evidence.quote: Texas' maternal mortality committee faces backlash for not reviewing deaths from first two years post-Dobbs
evidence.claim: the state declined to examine its own highest-salience period; caps A1 and A3 at 3 and drives EQ3 and I4 to 2

evidence.quote: The rate of sepsis shot up more than 50% for women hospitalized when they lost their pregnancies in the second trimester
evidence.claim: measured harm that the state itself did not measure; supports E3 and EQ3

evidence.quote: the risk of death or impairment does not have to be 'imminent' for doctors to act
evidence.claim: genuine legislative correction driven by patient testimony; primary support for E2, AB2 and B5

evidence.quote: These Women Couldn't Get Life-Saving Care. Now They're Changing the Law.
evidence.claim: documented case of low-power voices influencing a decision; supports A4 at 3

evidence.quote: After local agencies release Uvalde shooting records, calls continue for Texas DPS to follow suit
evidence.claim: state agency withheld while local agencies disclosed; caps A4 and drives EQ5 to 2

evidence.quote: Director Steve McCraw didn't have to resign, and in fact he got a big raise after the shooting
evidence.claim: absence of state-level consequence or repair after Uvalde; primary support for AB5 and EQ5

evidence.quote: At least 17 Texas National Guardsmen have died during the border mission, a number of them by suicide
evidence.claim: a state-directed mission depleting its own people; primary support for B1 and I3 at 2

evidence.quote: the rainy day fund projected to have $28.5 billion by the end of the budget in August 2027
evidence.claim: fiscal capacity existed alongside unmet need; primary support for AC4 and I4 Values Alignment at 2

evidence.quote: Governor Abbott signed SB 3 into law on September 5, 2025
evidence.claim: documented correction after a mass-casualty failure; supports AB2 at 3

evidence.quote: Attorney General Ken Paxton Sues Radical Network that Ships Abortion Drugs into Houston, San Antonio, Dallas, Fort Worth, Austin, El Paso
evidence.claim: enforcement posture toward those serving a disfavoured population; primary support for E3 at 2
